strings.tag: Hi [name], this is a test string from room003 - csi morgue.
Ah, the newest member of our team. Nice of you to drop by.
I have, and I'm ready to share my preliminary findings. What would you like to know?
White female, approximately 20 to 25 years old, 5 foot 5 inches tall, and about 115 pounds. Anything beyond that, you'll have to tell me.
There's significant decomposition, and it even looks like some animals have gotten to her. I'd say she's been dead two or three days.

I was able to get a DNA sample from her bone marrow, so you can try searching CODIS and the Missing Persons Database. Meanwhile, I'll X-ray her teeth for dental record comparisons. Check back later for the results.
NOT the fire - she was dead before it started. I found a stab wound in her chest. The weapon pierced her heart, so her death was quick.

A sharp object, probably about 5 inches in length. It left some striations on the rib cartilage; I left it out so you can make a cast. If you picked up the field kit that Grissom prepared for you, it should contain some impression gel that you can apply directly to the wound.
Have you finished the autopsy yet?
Who is this?
When did she die?
How can we identify her?
What was the cause of death?
What can you tell me about the murder weapon?
Thanks for the info.
Welcome back. Have you had any luck identifying our dead girl?
I was hoping to use her dental records, but the X-RAY showed no fillings or abnormalities, so comparing dental records could be difficult.
Well, it's chancy, but I thought facial reconstruction might help. I've asked an expert forensic artist to assist us.
Facial Reconstruction is a mix of forensic anthropology, anatomy, and artistry. It allows us to approximate a person's appearance from their skeletal remains.
I've asked an expert forensic artist, Taylor Wilkinson, to assist us. Go talk to her.
I can't find a DNA match. What else can we use to identify the body?
What can you tell me about the victim?
When did she die?
Is there another technique we can use?
What's facial reconstruction?
How do I do a facial reconstruction?
Thanks for the info.
I'm Taylor Wilkinson, expert forensic artist. Doc Robbins asked me to come help.
Maybe you should talk to Doc Robbins first. I'm sure he'll explain everything.
What are you here for?
I'll go talk to Doc.
I'm glad we'll be working together. Are you ready to begin?
The first step is to boil the skull, which gently removes the burnt skin and flesh. While that's going on, I need you to answer some questions for me. What sex is the victim?
That's not what I remember Doc saying earlier. Would you like to try again?
OK. And what is her approximate age?
And lastly, what was her racial background?
The skull is clean and we're ready to begin. The first step is to add Tissue Depth Markers. I've already added some, but would like you to finish.
These small wooden pegs indicate the average amount of muscle, skin and fat surrounding the bones. The depth depends on a person's age, sex and race.
Tissue depth depends on a person's age, sex and race. I've cut these to the average tissue depth for a 20-25 year old white female.
Yes.
No.
Male.
Female.
We can't tell.
We can't tell.
Caucasian
African-American
Asian
We can't tell.
What are tissue depth markers?
How do you know how long to cut them?
Show me how to attach the tissue depth markers.

DIRECTIONS

A total of 33 tissue depth markers are used in facial reconstruction. Dr. Wilkinson has already placed 23 of these markers and wants you to finish the job.

Compare the victim's skull with the reference diagrams to determine where to place the missing markers. Click the correct area to place the marker.
Rotate Skull
We will now attach various muscles and glands on the face. These will help to flesh out the face and give it the correct shape.
Of course not! I'll form the appropriate organ from modeling clay.
Are these real muscles and glands?
I want to help attach the muscles and glands.

DIRECTIONS

We need to attach 10 different muscles and structures to the skull.

You will be given a molded organ and a brief description of its function. Drag that organ to the correct location.
Function:
Name: Temporalis
This fan-shaped muscle gives the side of the head a smooth, rounded appearance.
Name: Masseter
This bulky, strong muscle fills out the cheek area. It helps us chew our food.
Name: Orbicularis Oris
This circular muscle forms the slit of the mouth and is the basis for the lips.
Name: Mentalis and Depressor Muscles
This group of muscles help to pull our lips downward when we frown.
Name: Orbicularis Oculi
This round muscle controls both sets of eyelids.
Name: Nasal Cartilage
The shape of this structure can be deduced from the shape of the nasal cavity and the surrounding bones.
Name: Zygomaticus Muscles
These muscles bring the corners of the mouth upwards when you smile.
Name: Occipitofrontalis
This large flat muscle can raise the eyebrows or wrinkle the forehead.
Name: Parotid Gland
This large gland suppies the saliva that helps food dissolve.
Name: Ears
The size and angle of the ears can be deduced from the surrounding bone, but we must guess at the details.
Well done. Lastly, we add skin and features. It would be best if I handle this part, and you watch.
Most of it I can deduce from the bone structure. For example, teeth height tells me about the thickness of the lips, and the width of the eye sockets tells me about the eye lids and eyebrows.
How do you know what the features look like?
I want to watch you work.
There we go - that's our victim. I'll give you a picture of the facial reconstruction to aid your investigation.
Brown is the most common eye color, and the wig I used is a very popular hair style. However, the victim's actual eye and hair color may be different - be alert!
Facial Reconstruction works some of the time, but not always. Good training and working in close collaboration with forensic scientists increases the success rate.
How do you know what color eyes and hair to use?
Are you sure this is accurate?
Thank you for your help.
